content.full_text:
Memorandum

To: Tracy Fletcher  
Political Reform Division

Date: Dec. 7, 1983

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
Jeanne Pritchard

Subject: Lobbying Reporting

This is to confirm our telephone conversation, and to clarify some of the issues which have recently arisen regarding the "period covered" and the "cumulative period beginning" for the first report required to be filed by a lobbyist.

The Commission's interpretation of the law has been that the period covered by the first report is the beginning of the calendar quarter in which the lobbyist first qualified, and the cumulative period for the first report begins with January 1 of the current year. My understanding is that the Political Reform Division has applied this interpretation in determining the correct periods a first report is to cover.

The staff has recently reviewed this question and has determined that, for the first report required to be filed, both the period covered and the cumulative period should begin with the first day of the calendar quarter in which the lobbyist first qualified. The staff has proposed an amendment to Regulation Section 18616(e), which clarifies this (copy enclosed). The regulation will be considered by the Commission at its January meeting and, if approved, the regulation will go into effect early next year.

We realize that there has been considerable confusion on the part of filers about what the appropriate dates are. You have asked us what approach the Political Reform Division should take in requesting amendments to reports which do not contain the proper period covered or cumulative period beginning date. We think it would be appropriate to accept either the first day of the calendar quarter or January 1, for both the cumulative period and the period covered date, until the regulation goes into effect. Once the regulation has been approved by the Commission, we will notify filers that the correct beginning date is the first day of the calendar quarter.

I apologize for the confusion and inconvenience this has caused.
